ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cassava chip entry (FOODON_03301710); no specific spicy/sambal-coated variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers fried starchy snacks from root vegetables; no dedicated spicy tapioca chip code exists. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for spicy tapioca chips; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: seasoned with sambal, salt, and honey

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of spicy tapioca chips is approximately 30 g (a small snack pack or a generous handful), providing about 140 kcal, 7.2 g fat, 17.4 g carbohydrate, and 188 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Tapioca Chips — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spicy Tapioca Chips (cr_id F12050006). All nine reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from palm-oil frying context (see notes).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; tapioca / cassava chip analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy sambal-coated tapioca chips. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried tapioca/cassava-based snacks and peer-reviewed data for cassava starch products (GI range 65–75). Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 16.9) / 100 ≈ 11.8. Honey and sambal coating may marginally elevate postprandial glucose response. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 626 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g hypertension hard gate
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid spicy tapioca chips; a single 30 g serving delivers 188 mg sodium, and regular consumption will meaningfully elevate daily sodium intake beyond the 2,000 mg/day target recommended by the Singapore HPB and WHO.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 20.59 g/100 g and high-GI tapioca starch (GI ≈ 70) drive rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid spicy tapioca chips entirely; the combination of 20.6 g sugar/100 g, 58 g total carbohydrate/100 g from high-GI tapioca starch, and a glycemic load of approximately 11.8 per 30 g serving will cause significant postprandial blood glucose spikes that are incompatible with glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~10.5 g/100 g from palm oil frying), high sodium (626 mg/100 g), and high sugar (20.59 g/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid spicy tapioca chips; the saturated fat from palm-oil deep-frying raises LDL cholesterol, the high sodium load promotes arterial hypertension, and the elevated sugar content contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g from palm oil frying exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; chili (nightshade) may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid spicy tapioca chips; palm-oil frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and delivers high saturated fat that promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, while chili-based sambal may trigger solanine-related inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy tapioca chips (Malay: Kerepek Ubi Pedas; Chinese: 辣木薯片) are a popular Southeast Asian snack made from thinly sliced cassava (tapioca) that is deep-fried in palm oil until crisp, then coated with a seasoned sauce mixture of sambal (chili paste), salt, and honey. The result is a crunchy, intensely flavoured snack with a sweet-spicy-salty profile. The chips are a staple snack in Malay culinary culture across Singapore and Malaysia, commonly consumed at breakfast or as an afternoon snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g the chips deliver 466 kcal, 23.9 g fat (of which approximately 10.5 g is saturated fat from palm oil), 58 g carbohydrate, 20.6 g sugar, 4.2 g dietary fibre, and 626 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), driven by the refined tapioca starch base and honey coating. Clinically, this snack is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (sodium exceeds 600 mg/100 g), Type 2 Diabetes (sugar >20 g/100 g and high GI), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and sodium), or rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory saturated fat and nightshade chili). The food is classified as ultra-processed, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Calcium is 92 mg/100 g and iron is 1.56 mg/100 g, providing modest micronutrient contributions.
common_user_queries: Is kerepek ubi pedas high in sodium?
Is kerepek ubi suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 with exact match to cr_id F12050006. Saturated fat is estimated from palm-oil frying context and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, appropriately flagged.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.5 g/100 g) is estimated based on palm oil as the primary frying medium; the HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown separately. This value is consistent with published palm-oil frying data for similar snack products. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific spicy tapioca chip preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried cassava/tapioca-based snacks; no direct GI measurement for sambal-coated tapioca chips was found in approved sources. 4. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' present in the input record has been removed as tapioca starch is a refined carbohydrate and does not qualify as wholegrain. 5. Honey inclusion means this product is not vegan despite being plant-based. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
